text: The surgeon uses a bipolar grasper and a Maryland dissector to manipulate the thymoma, which is clearly visible during the right-sided approach for the thymectomy.

The surgeon utilizes a three-port approach from the right side of the chest, including two 5mm ports and a 10mm port for the endoscope. The inferior portion of the thymus is located just above the diaphragm. Dissection begins off the back of the sternum, maintaining a distance from the thymus and thymoma to ensure a good margin.

The surgeon uses small, slim 5mm instruments, including a spatula or hook for monopolar diathermy, to carefully remove thymic fat during the thymectomy. The bipolar energy tool is preferred for its precision near the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon begins dissection near the diaphragm, moving towards the pericardium while carefully taking thymic fat with the specimen. Small branches are visible coming off the reamer, and the right internal mammary artery is seen at the top of the screen.

The surgeon begins dissection approximately one centimeter medial to the right internal mammary artery, using precise instrument control with the CMR system. The icons indicating the instrument base units are visible on the screen.

The surgical team uses a color-coordinated instrument setup, with the left base unit in purple housing the bipolar grasper and the right base unit in green. A third cyan base unit is visible but not utilized, as the Maryland dissector is removed from the surgical field.

The surgical team uses five millimeter ports to facilitate instrument exchange during the thymectomy. The bedside assistant employs a harmonic scalpel to manage any vessels requiring energy application.

The surgeon dissects over the top of the pericardium, moving towards the pleura on the left-hand side, while ensuring a large margin. The dissection progresses under the sternum, with the reamer visibly pulsating. The goal is to reach the left internal mammary artery.

The surgeon carefully dissects up the chest, maintaining a safe distance from the thymoma. The right internal mammary vein is visible to the left of the Maryland dissector, serving as a landmark. The pleura is being taken down with a generous margin, while the right internal mammary vein is followed down towards its insertion into the superior vena cava.

The surgeon carefully dissects approximately half a centimeter medial to the phrenic nerve using bipolar energy, minimizing energy dissipation to protect the nerve. The superior vena cava (SVC) is visible below the phrenic nerve, and a small lymph node is included in the sample.

The surgeon uses grasping forceps to peel the thymus from the pericardium, lifting forcefully to separate the tissue. The pericardium and diaphragm insertion are visible, and the surgeon follows along the phrenic nerve from below upwards, utilizing the grip strength of the five-millimeter instruments.

The surgeon identifies a lymph node in the surgical field, which will be kept with the specimen for classification purposes. The dissection is completed from above downwards, revealing a clean pericardium with no visible fat.

The surgeon navigates the dissection into the neck area, following the innominate vein across. Visible landmarks include the white internal mammary vein and the superior vena cava (SVC). The surgeon carefully examines a small vessel potentially originating from the right internal mammary vein.

The surgeon identifies the blood supply to the thymus, noting inferior blood vessels and lateral vessels from the reamer vein and artery. A possible tributary from the reamer vein is addressed using a harmonic device and a 5mm clip applicator, with clips placed on either side by the assistant.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to safely divide a small vein in the middle of the surgical field. The green instrument unit with the Maryland dissector is used to carefully dissect the thymus from the innominate vein, providing a clear view of the vein just beyond the Maryland instrument. The surgeon prepares to identify the thymic vein, which often drains into the innominate vein.

The surgeon identifies a vessel that requires clipping or division using the harmonic scalpel. The pericardium is visible with the aorta beneath it, as the surgeon carefully dissects soft tissue, ensuring no fat remains and all tissue is removed with the thymoma.

The camera provides an excellent view as the surgeon uses the Versius hand controls to maneuver. They search for vascular structures from the reamer or innominate vein entering the thymus. Two potential vascular structures are identified. The right-hand arm is swapped out, removing the Maryland instrument, and a harmonic device is inserted to safely divide a potential vessel from the green vein.

The surgeon addresses the left and right superior horns of the thymus, visible just below the harmonic device, as they extend over the innominate vein. The left-sided pleura is visible in the distance above the harmonic. The surgeon uses the left hand to pull down the thymus, aided by CO₂ insufflation creating space between the sternum and thymus. The thymoma is being divided from the pericardium, appearing relatively benign, without needing to dissect into the pericardium.

The surgeon tracks up into the right superior horn of the thymus, preparing to safely divide it. The innominate vein is visible below the Maryland dissector, with the thymic vein emerging from the innominate. The surgeon points to the innominate vein and thymic vein, positioning instruments on either side to facilitate safe division.

The surgeon clips the thymic vein using a surgical instrument, preparing to divide it with a harmonic device. The Maryland instrument is removed, and the camera view shows the rotation and unwinding of instruments, facilitated by the joystick controls.

The surgeon uses the Versys robotic system to pull the superior horn of the thymus out of the neck briskly, allowing for the division of the thyro-thymic ligament with a harmonic device. The procedure reveals a small artery and vein supplying the ligament.

The surgeon addresses the left superior horn of the thymus, carefully pulling it out from the neck while staying medial to the pleura to protect the lemur artery and vein. The assistant uses a harmonic device to aid in the dissection, ensuring clear visualization and safe manipulation of the tissue.

The surgeon uses a Maryland retractor to pull the thymus into the right chest, dividing the left side of the thymus from the left pleura. The clean pericardium is visible below the Maryland, and small tissue remnants are removed while keeping the fat attached to the thymus. The left pleura is entered early in the procedure, with CO₂ insufflation maintaining space in the chest. The left and right superior horns of the thymus are already extracted from the neck, and the thymus appears more mobile.

The surgeon uses bipolar diathermy to continue dissection along the left pleura, joining the previous dissection performed in the neck. The procedure utilizes the Versius system with three ports, including two 5mm ports and an end-escape camera, demonstrating the system's efficiency for thymectomy.

The surgeon carefully pulls fat away from under the left phrenic nerve, ensuring the nerve remains safe while brushing away connective tissue. The superior vena cava (SVC) is visible, with the innominate vein extending into the distance. The Maryland instrument is used to press the innominate vein, while the left superior horn is dissected off, dividing the thyrothymic ligament to complete the dissection.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the thymus, revealing the pericardium with the underlying aorta, innominate vein, and reamer vein, all devoid of fatty tissue. The superior vena cava (SVC) and phrenic nerve are clearly dissected. A large thymoma, measuring seven centimeters, is visible. The surgeon enlarges the right-sided port, places the thymoma in a retrieval bag, and removes it from the surgical field.
